title: T-091 amendment widens shell-literal scope to basename match — tools/ is not enforced
description: The orchestrator amendment to T-091 swapped 'kind:shell literal confined to runPreflightProbe.ts' for an ALLOWED set {runPreflightProbe.ts, preflight.ts, manifest.ts} matched against `path.basename(f)`. Because the comparison is on basename only, a hypothetical future regression that adds a NEW tool file named (e.g.) `tools/manifest.ts` containing kind:'shell' would slip past T-091 entirely. The synthesis-notes correctly observes that the load-bearing intent is 'no NEW tool emits shell actions' and that T-148 in runPreflightProbe.test.ts still enforces the per-tool-file claim within mcp/server/src/tools/ (only runPreflightProbe.ts is allowed). So the invariant is still covered in aggregate, but T-091 standalone is now permissive about file location.
impact: Non-load-bearing in practice (T-148 catches the load-bearing tools/ regression). Future cycles should re-tighten T-091 by checking path.relative against an absolute file allowlist — e.g. ALLOWED = new Set(['mcp/server/src/preflight.ts', 'mcp/server/src/probes/manifest.ts', 'mcp/server/src/tools/runPreflightProbe.ts']) — so renaming or relocating an emitter is caught.
recommendation: Optionally tighten the ALLOWED set to absolute relative paths instead of basenames; or simply note the tightening as a cycle-6 follow-up since T-148 already covers the load-bearing per-tool claim.

title: Snapshot rotation collides on millisecond-fast successive rung-3 calls — A7 append-only claim is not enforced under racy timing
description: runAutoWrite rotates an existing .bak to `.bak.<ISO-timestamp-no-colons>` using `new Date().toISOString().replace(/:/g, '')`. The timestamp has millisecond precision. A third (or any subsequent) rung-3 invocation that fires within the same millisecond as a previous rotation would compute the same rotated path, and `fsPromises.rename(bakPath, rotatedPath)` would silently overwrite the prior rotated archive on POSIX (rename onto an existing file is destructive). The contract A7 claims 'snapshot files are append-only across rung-3 attempts', but T-018 (requestHint) and T-052 (ladder) only exercise TWO rung-3 calls — the byte-fidelity assertions check only the first rotation, never the second. The implementation does NOT add a counter or recheck collision before rename, so no test catches sub-ms back-to-back rotations.
impact: Real-world impact is negligible (a student will not press rung-3 twice within 1ms), but the test-vs-impl pairing for A7 is technically loose: the contract's append-only claim is asserted by tests that only cover one rotation step, leaving a gap that the impl quietly papers over with rename's overwrite semantics.
recommendation: Either (a) add a third rung-3 invocation in T-018/T-052 with a vi.useFakeTimers() that holds the system clock at a fixed instant, asserting both rotated archives co-exist (would require the impl to add a collision-resolving suffix); or (b) explicitly note in the contract that rotation is best-effort and update A7 wording. Option (a) makes the invariant tight; option (b) downgrades A7 to a documentation note.

title: A6 source-grep tests cover only requestHint.ts and ladder.ts — contract scope says all engine code
description: Contract A6 specifies the literal `auto_completed = false` 'matches in zero lines' across `mcp/server/src/**/*.ts` (excluding the schema's default-on-load branch and tests). T-039 (requestHint.test.ts:997) and T-040 (ladder.test.ts:674) only inspect those two cycle-5 files. A regression in any other engine file (e.g. nextSpot.ts, verifySpot.ts, phaseEngine.ts) that introduces an `auto_completed = false` re-write would not be caught. Today no such occurrences exist (verified with `grep -rn 'auto_completed\s*=\s*false' mcp/server/src/`), so the gap is theoretical, but the tests do not enforce A6's stated scope.
impact: Currently unobservable — no engine code outside the two cycle-5 files writes `auto_completed = false`. But future cycles that touch the cursor-advance or state-merge logic could regress AC-5.4 (auto_completed permanence) and the existing source-grep tests would not flag it.
recommendation: Add a single source-grep test that walks all .ts under `mcp/server/src/` (excluding `schemas/state.ts`'s default-on-load branch and tests/) and asserts zero `auto_completed = false` literals. This widens the AC-5.4 coverage to match the contract scope.

title: harness.lesson E-004 traversal bypasses MCP transport for requestHint when verifyStub is set
description: When the harness has `verifyStub` installed (via `withVerifyStub`), `callTool('requestHint', ...)` short-circuits to call `runRequestHint` directly with the stub spawn. The MCP client/server round-trip is skipped. T-096/T-097/T-098 (the E-004 traversal cases) all install withVerifyStub before calling requestHint, so those tests do NOT exercise the MCP wire shape for requestHint. This is reasonable engineering (otherwise the rung-3 dispatch would shell out to pnpm), and T-107 covers the gap by calling `harness.callTool('requestHint', ...)` WITHOUT a verifyStub, asserting the SDK does not reply 'tool not found'. So in aggregate the MCP registration is end-to-end-tested. Worth noting because the question 'does E-004 drive through the MCP transport?' has the nuanced answer 'no for the verify-pass path; yes via T-107 smoke marker'.
impact: None operationally — T-107 closes the gap. Documenting this is useful for future reviewers who want to verify that requestHint's wire boundary is exercised.
recommendation: No change needed. If a future cycle wants stricter MCP coverage, add an E-004 variant that drives only rung-1 and rung-2 (which have no verifySpot dispatch) without the verifyStub, so the full MCP round-trip is exercised on the happy path.

title: T-094 back-compat test does not exercise auto_write_attempted=true preservation on load
description: T-094 verifies that a legacy state.json without `auto_write_attempted` loads to kind:'ok' with the field defaulted to false. It does NOT assert that an EXISTING `auto_write_attempted: true` value is preserved through load — which is the case that exercises append-only durability (a student who completed rung-3 in cycle 4 expects that flag to survive). The schema implementation (`mcp/server/src/schemas/state.ts:94`) handles both cases via `typeof r['auto_write_attempted'] === 'boolean' ? r['auto_write_attempted'] : false`, so today the impl is correct. But the test only covers the absent-field path.
impact: If a future schema-normalization refactor accidentally clobbered an existing true value to false (e.g., by always defaulting), no test would catch it. AC-5.4 (auto_completed permanent) is durability-tested via T-097 (session restart), but the schema-load-level back-compat test for auto_write_attempted is one-sided.
recommendation: Add a one-line companion case to T-094 with `auto_write_attempted: true` in the legacy shape and assert `result.value.ladder['p1-spot-1'].auto_write_attempted === true` after load.
